epigraph.text:
I was there when he set the heavens in place, when 
he marked out the horizon on the face of the deep.

content.text:
Geometry begins with the simplest objects: points, lines, and the 
angles formed where lines meet. These basic building blocks combine 
to create all the shapes we see in the world.

content.title: Point
content.content:
A **point** is a location in space. It has no size, 
only position. We name points with capital letters: $A$, $B$, $C$.

content.title: Line
content.content:
A **line** is a straight path that extends infinitely 
in both directions. We write $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$ to denote the 
line through points $A$ and $B$.

content.title: Line Segment
content.content:
A **line segment** is the part of a line 
between two points (including those points). We write $\overline{AB}$ 
for the segment from $A$ to $B$.

content.title: Ray
content.content:
A **ray** starts at one point and extends infinitely 
in one direction. We write $\overrightarrow{AB}$ for the ray starting 
at $A$ and passing through $B$.

content.title: Angle
content.content:
An **angle** is formed by two rays that share a 
common endpoint. The shared endpoint is called the **vertex**, 
and the two rays are called the **sides** of the angle.

content.text: We name angles in several ways:

items: By three points: $\angle ABC$ (the vertex is always in the middle)
By the vertex alone: $\angle B$ (when only one angle is at that vertex)

content.content:
Just as angles are formed at the meeting point of two rays, our lives 
often change direction at key "vertex" moments—decisions where 
paths diverge or converge.

content.text:
Angles are measured in **degrees** (symbol: $°$). A full rotation 
is $360°$, half a rotation is $180°$, and a quarter rotation is $90°$.

content.title: Using a protractor
content.problem:
To measure an angle with a protractor:

1. Place the center mark of the protractor on the vertex

2. Align the baseline with one side of the angle

3. Read the degree measure where the other side crosses the scale

4. Use the correct scale (0° should be on your baseline)

content.title: Types of Angles
content.content:
- **Acute angle**: measures less than $90°$

- **Right angle**: measures exactly $90°$ (marked with a small square)

- **Obtuse angle**: measures greater than $90°$ but less than $180°$

- **Straight angle**: measures exactly $180°$ (forms a straight line)

content.title: Classifying angles
content.solution:
1. $45° < 90°$, so it is **acute**

2. $90°$ exactly, so it is a **right angle**

3. $90° < 120° < 180°$, so it is **obtuse**

4. $180°$ exactly, so it is a **straight angle**

content.title: Scripture
content.content:
The right angle ($90°$) is fundamental to construction. When Amos 
received a vision of God with "a plumb line in his hand" (Amos 7:7), 
it symbolized measuring Israel against God's righteous standard—a 
perfect vertical, a right angle with the earth.

marginNotes.text:
In the beginning God created the heavens 
and the earth.

I will make justice the measuring line 
and righteousness the plumb line.

I consider all your precepts right; I 
hate every wrong path.

marginNotes.author: Euclid
marginNotes.text: A point is that which has no part.

marginNotes.author: Plato
marginNotes.text: Geometry will draw the soul toward truth.
